questions.question: Explain the concept of distinguishable states with an example.
keyPoints: States p and q are distinguishable if there exists an input string w such that one state accepts w and the other rejects it
Example: In DFA for a*b, state after reading 'a' is distinguishable from start state because 'a' is accepted by one but not the other
If no such string exists, states are equivalent (indistinguishable)

questions.question: What is a dead state in a DFA? Why is it important in minimization?
keyPoints: Dead state (or trap/sink state) is a non-accepting state that transitions to itself for all input symbols
It represents automatic rejection of any string leading to it
Important in minimization: Incomplete DFAs are completed by adding dead states before minimization
Dead states can often be merged or eliminated if they don't contribute to acceptance

questions.question: Describe the initial partition step in DFA minimization.
keyPoints: The set of states Q is partitioned into two initial subsets
Subset 1: All accepting (final) states F
Subset 2: All non-accepting states Q - F
This initial partition is based on the fundamental fact that an accepting state can never be equivalent to a non-accepting state

questions.question: What is the Myhill-Nerode theorem? How is it related to DFA minimization?
keyPoints: Myhill-Nerode theorem provides characterization of regular languages
It states that a language L is regular iff the number of equivalence classes of the Myhill-Nerode relation is finite
The minimum number of states in any DFA recognizing L equals the number of equivalence classes
This gives a theoretical lower bound on the number of states in a minimal DFA

questions.question: Explain the partition refinement algorithm for DFA minimization.
keyPoints: Start with initial partition: {F}, {Q-F}
For each group, check if states in it can be distinguished by their transition behavior
If states in same group go to different groups on same input, split the group
Repeat until partition doesn't change (no new splits)
Final groups represent equivalent states that can be merged

questions.question: What is the table-filling method for finding distinguishable state pairs?
keyPoints: Create a table with all state pairs as entries
Initially mark pairs (p,q) as distinguishable if one is accepting and other is not
For each unmarked pair, for each input symbol, check if transitions lead to marked pairs
If yes, mark the pair as distinguishable
Iterate until no new pairs are marked; unmarked pairs are equivalent

questions.question: Define equivalence of DFAs. When are two DFAs said to be equivalent?
keyPoints: Two DFAs are equivalent if they recognize the same language
Formally, DFA M1 and M2 are equivalent if for every string w, either both accept w or both reject w
Equivalence of states within a DFA is different - it refers to states having identical behavior
DFA equivalence can be checked by comparing their minimal forms

questions.question: Apply the minimization algorithm to the given DFA and find the minimal DFA: M = ({q0,q1,q2,q3}, {a,b}, δ, q0, {q2}) where δ: δ(q0,a)=q1, δ(q0,b)=q2, δ(q1,a)=q1, δ(q1,b)=q3, δ(q2,a)=q1, δ(q2,b)=q2, δ(q3,a)=q1, δ(q3,b)=q2
keyPoints: Initial partition: {q2} (accepting), {q0,q1,q3} (non-accepting)
Check q0,q1,q3: On 'a', all go to q1 (same group). On 'b', q0→q2(distinguishable), q1→q3(same group), q3→q2(distinguishable)
Since q0 and q3 go to q2 (accepting) and q1 goes to q3 (non-accepting), split {q0,q1,q3}
New partition: {q2}, {q0,q3}, {q1}
Check {q0,q3}: On 'a', both go to q1. On 'b', both go to q2 (same transitions)
Final partition: {q2}, {q0,q3}, {q1} - these are equivalent classes
Minimal DFA will have 3 states representing these classes

questions.question: Explain the step-by-step procedure for minimizing a DFA. Why is it necessary to minimize a DFA?
keyPoints: Step 1: Remove unreachable states (not reachable from start state)
Step 2: If incomplete, complete the DFA by adding dead state
Step 3: Create initial partition into accepting and non-accepting states
Step 4: Iteratively refine partition based on transition behavior
Step 5: Stop when partition stabilizes; merge states in each final subset
Need for minimization: Reduces memory required, faster processing, easier to analyze, identifies redundant states, optimal representation of the language

questions.question: Using the table-filling method, find all distinguishable state pairs and equivalent state pairs for DFA: M = ({q0,q1,q2}, {0,1}, δ, q0, {q1}) where δ(q0,0)=q0, δ(q0,1)=q1, δ(q1,0)=q2, δ(q1,1)=q1, δ(q2,0)=q2, δ(q2,1)=q1
keyPoints: State pairs: (q0,q1), (q0,q2), (q1,q2)
Initially mark (q0,q1) as q1 is accepting, q0 is not
Check (q1,q2): q1 is accepting, q2 is not - mark as distinguishable
Check (q0,q2): Initially unmarked, check transitions
On '0': q0→q0, q2→q2 - both go to unmarked pair
On '1': q0→q1(marked), q2→q1(marked) - both go to marked pair, so (q0,q2) is distinguishable
All pairs distinguishable, no equivalent states exist
Minimal DFA has 3 states (cannot be reduced)

questions.question: State and explain Myhill-Nerode theorem. How can it be used to determine the minimum number of states in a DFA?
keyPoints: Myhill-Nerode Theorem: A language L ⊆ Σ* is regular iff the number of equivalence classes of the relation R_L is finite, where xR_L y iff for all z∈Σ*, xz∈L iff yz∈L
Equivalence classes represent distinct 'futures' or behaviors needed to recognize the language
The minimum number of states in any DFA recognizing L equals the number of equivalence classes
If there are k equivalence classes, no DFA can recognize L with fewer than k states
The minimal DFA (if it exists) has exactly one state per equivalence class
This provides a theoretical lower bound and helps verify if a given DFA is minimal
